texto: I always remember your own grandmother, she continued, nodding her head, old Mrs. Taylor. She died on a Christmas Night.Oh, I said shivering. I wouldn't like to die on a Christmas Night. A good night to die, she smiled; they say that the gates of heaven are open on Christmas Night.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Alice Taylor: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a familia. O trecho “I always remember your own grandmother, she continued, nodding her head, old Mrs. Taylor. She die…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que familia, christmas, death pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de boa-noite e luto.

frase_original: I always remember your own grandmother, she continued, nodding her head, old Mrs. Taylor. She died on a Christmas Night.Oh, I said shivering. I wouldn't like to die on a Christmas Night. A good night to die, she smiled; they say that the gates of heaven are open on Christmas Night.

texto: Mrs. Casey, do you love Christmas? Well you know, she answered reflectively, Christmas can be a sad time for people too. It's a remembering time for us older ones. We remember the people who are gone.Oh, I never thought of that, I told her in surprise.Well that's youth for you, she said; you don't start to look back over your shoulder until there is something to look back at, and around Christmas I tend to think of the Christmases past and the people gone with them.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Alice Taylor que provoca limites e escolhas conscientes. Ao questionar “Mrs. Casey, do you love Christmas? Well you know, she answered reflectively, Christmas can be a s…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, christmas, rememberance-of-things-past, com foco em natal e termino.
